content:
Welcome back to the 2022 ICT mentorship on YouTube. This lesson will focus on intraday order flow and
understanding the daily range.

We will be looking at TradingView.com, which can be used for free or with an affordable membership. This
platform allows you to view the same charts I use in my mentorship.
This lecture will predominantly teach the elements of the E-mini, setting up your daily range, and intraday
layouts. I will also discuss daily profiles, comparing my teachings with what is generally available online to
help you decide if this pursuit is worthwhile.

The concepts taught today are directly linked to index futures, specifically:
- S&P (ES)
- NASDAQ (NQ)
- Dow (YM)
- Russell 2000 (RTY)
The ideas of trading the Asian range and similar concepts are not applicable to these markets and will not
be taught in this mentorship. Focus on the methods provided.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING FUTURES CONTRACT EXPIRATION AND SYMBOLS
content:
Index Futures contracts trade with expiration dates. The delivery month codes are:
- H: March
- M: June
- U: September
- Z: December
These are the only four months index futures (E-mini S&P, E-mini NASDAQ, E-mini Dow) trade on. The
year follows the month code.
For example, on TradingView, ES H2022 represents the E-mini S&P 500 contract for March 2022.

Contracts expire on the third Friday of the delivery month (e.g., third Friday of March for an H contract).
- You should not trade after expiration.
- The 'front month' or 'current month' refers to the nearest contract month.
- The 'next month out' refers to the contract month immediately following the front month.

section_title: MONITORING OPEN INTEREST FOR CONTRACT SELECTION
content:
To determine which contract to trade, monitor open interest:
- One week before expiration (the third Friday of the delivery month), begin monitoring open interest.
- Roll over to the month that has a larger open interest number.
- Example: If the June contract has larger open interest than March, trade June for better liquidity.
This ensures you are trading the contract month with the larger liquidity pool. The sequence repeats
annually: March, June, September, December.

This week's lesson focuses on the intraday framework. Next week, we will cover bias and specific entry
techniques. You will have homework assignments at the end of this video related to market profiles.
We will assume a bullish bias for today's examples, though proof of this bias and execution will be provided
later. It is crucial to follow the lesson pace and complete homework assignments to build ingrained
understanding. Your study time is where the majority of learning occurs.

The 15-minute time frame is the 'bellwether time frame'. Here, you look for:
- Key highs and lows
- Imbalances like Fair Value Gaps
- Order blocks (though the focus in this mentorship is fair value gaps due to their repetitive and easy-to
- spot nature).
This time frame helps frame your day. For charting, use vertical lines in TradingView or your platform.

section_title: SETTING UP INTRADAY ZONES ON YOUR CHART
content:
To set up your intraday chart:
- Place a vertical line at 8:30 AM New York local time.
- Place another vertical line at 12:00 PM (Noon) New York local time.
- Place a third vertical line at 1:00 PM New York local time.
- Place a final vertical line at 4:00 PM New York local time.
These lines establish equal distance in time for the morning session, the lunch hour, and the afternoon
session. It is critical that your TradingView chart is set to New York local time for all calibrations to align with
my teachings and community charts.

section_title: NEW YORK LOCAL TIME AND THE 'NO TRADE' LUNCH HOUR
content:
All time references are New York local time. Setting your chart to this ensures consistency with the
mentorship.
- The hour from noon to 1:00 PM New York time is a no-trade time period. Do not trade during this time,
even in demo accounts. More reasons for this will be explained later in the mentorship, but trust this rule for
now. This period can be volatile or consolidate, neither is ideal for participation.

section_title: DAILY RANGE BOUNDARIES AND OPTIMAL TRADING TIMES
content:
Your daily range on an intraday basis is now set up:
- Morning trade session: 8:30 AM (due to news events) to noon.
- Preferably, aim to exit or manage trades around 11:00 AM. The goal is to be positioned before 11:00 AM
and ride the move into the New York lunch hour.
- The afternoon session (post-1:00 PM) will be discussed further when we delve into daily profiles (not
volume profiles).

The afternoon session will be taught to provide a complete daily treatise on the entire daily range of
indices. This will offer a far better chance of success using the provided information, supported by actual
trade executions.
Many questions have arisen regarding what highs and lows to look for prior to 8:30 AM as catalysts for stop
hunts. I appreciate all feedback in the comment section as it helps refine future lessons and explanations.

section_title: IDENTIFYING PRE-8:30 SWING HIGHS AND LOWS
content:
Prior to 8:30 AM (to the left of the 8:30 vertical line on a 15-minute time frame), identify the most significant
or obvious swing high and swing low.
- Swing High: A candle with a lower high to its left and a lower high to its right (a three-candle pattern). The
close of the candles does not matter. Buy-side liquidity (buy stops) rests above a swing high.
- Swing Low: A candle with a higher low to its left and a higher low to its right (a three-candle pattern). The
close of the candles does not matter. Sell-side liquidity (sell stops) rests below a swing low.
These swing points are where liquidity is placed.

Once you have identified these key levels on your 15-minute chart, you can drop down to your first lower
time frame for entry the 5-minute chart. All your annotations will transpose to the 5-minute chart without
being lost.

section_title: THE THREE DRIVES PATTERN AND STOP HUNTS
content:
Observe the price action around old highs. If you see relative equal highs or multiple short-term highs
going higher (a 'three drives pattern' or 'three little endings' as described by Linda Raschke and Larry
Connor), this indicates liquidity building.
- A three drives pattern is a swing high, a higher swing high, and another higher swing high. The market
repeatedly presses up.
- This pattern is significant when forming into an old high on any time frame (it's universal).
- Each time a swing high is created and price moves down, bears attempt to sell, placing buy stops above
the previous high. These stops are taken, building liquidity.
- Informed investors ('smart money') will be establishing short positions during this process, anticipating a
market break.
DISPLACEMENT:

section_title: THE ELEPHANT IN THE SWIMMING POOL
content:
Displacement is crucial. When price goes above an old high and then trades down below it, you need to
see an 'obvious displacement'. Think of an elephant falling into a children's swimming pool the water is
clearly displaced.
- You don't want to see a weak, 'anemic' move lower after a high is taken.
- An energetic, animated movement lower confirms displacement.
- Once this clear displacement occurs, then you can look for a Fair Value Gap (FVG) for a potential short
entry. If price trades back up into the FVG, you can look to go short.

section_title: RULES FOR ENGAGEMENT AND RISK MANAGEMENT
content:
There's a process for learning, and it's good to be critical, but trust there are rules for a reason. These
patterns can sometimes fail, leading to losing trades due to misinterpretation or unexpected news events.
- Always have a stop loss.
- Practice sound money management.
- Limiting your risk is crucial; do not leave your losses open to the market's determination.
If you don't see higher highs forming, but a steady run up, anticipate the old high to be taken out by a small
margin, followed by an energetic, displaced move lower. This is your cue to look for the FVG.

section_title: IDENTIFYING FAIR VALUE GAPS (FVG) AFTER DISPLACEMENT
content:
After a swing high is taken out and an energetic break below it occurs:
- Look for a clear Fair Value Gap (FVG) formed by a smashing down movement.
- The low, the high, and if price trades back up into that FVG, you look for a short entry.
- Target the sell-side liquidity resting below the swing low identified prior to 8:30 AM.

Consider the example:
- Relative equal highs created, indicating buy-side liquidity (buy stops) resting above.
- Right before the lunch hour, price slightly goes above these highs, then trades down, entering the
noon-1:00 PM no-trade zone.
- Post-lunch (after 1:00 PM), monitor for indications of higher prices. The market may want to reach for the
buy-side liquidity pools that were not tagged earlier, as many short sellers' stops are resting there. This
leads to an aggressive bullish run into the close, often with small retracements followed by immediate runs.

Your homework is to analyze how the day is designed:
- A morning move.
- A lunch hour (no trading).
- An afternoon move.
Go back through your charts and:
- Describe the morning trend: bullish, bearish, or consolidation.
- Describe the afternoon trend. Sometimes, it's a continuation; other times, a reversal. A 'measured move'
means the afternoon duplicates the morning's range (e.g., 200 points in morning, another 200 in afternoon
for 400 total).
- Study daily charts from previous days to find big moves, especially when consolidation precedes a trend.

section_title: AFTERNOON SESSION ACCELERATION AND DAILY BIAS
content:
The afternoon session often sees accelerated moves into the close, particularly around 20 minutes to 4:00
PM and 10 minutes to 4:00 PM, and at 4:00 PM itself. This is driven by 'Market On Close' (MOC) orders,
with algorithms spitting out aggressive pricing to force traders to cover or exit.
- If you know your daily bias (bullish or bearish range expansion), you can anticipate these moves.
- If your analysis suggests a daily candle will expand higher, look for trades in the afternoon based on
morning logic. This is similar to a 'London low in a buy day' for Forex, but calibrated to New York time.

It is paramount that your charts, especially on TradingView, are set to New York local time. If your charts
are set to your local time zone, everything you learn here will be miscalibrated and lead to confusion.
Calibrate your charts now; the phenomena discussed repeat the same way every single day.

For homework, outline the morning session's character and the afternoon session's character. Note:
- Bullish morning, continuation afternoon (potentially a measured move).
- Bullish morning, reversal afternoon.
- Consolidation morning, trend afternoon (higher or lower).
This work is crucial for learning. Compare this approach to others who focus on very small moves with
large contract sizes and high anxiety. My goal is to teach you how to understand deeper price mechanics.

Do not chase small tick movements with high leverage. Instead, focus on understanding the market's intent.
For the afternoon session:
- Look for swing highs and swing lows after 1:30 PM New York time. This is the earliest I start trading the
afternoon.
- There is an algorithm macro that starts running at 1:30 PM, creating movement in equity markets.
- Apply the same stop hunt context as the morning session: look for clean, straight-line levels that tend to
be disrupted.

section_title: AFTERNOON SESSION STOP HUNTS AND BUY PROGRAMS
content:
If you anticipate higher prices and see a swing low violated after 1:30 PM (e.g., a clean level with relative
equal lows):
- This small stop hunt (running through sell stops) can initiate a 'buy program'.
- A buy program is when algorithms continuously offer higher prices ('spooling'), regardless of volume. This
is market manipulation, and knowing its likely action provides an edge.
- Buy the sell stops that are resting below the violated swing low and expect the next high liquidity pool to
be targeted.

These market dynamics tend to repeat a majority of the time, not every day, but often enough to be
profitable if conditions align. If you see the 'fingerprints' of these patterns, it's likely to pan out.
Two primary entry patterns:
- Stop hunt violation: Wait for a swing low (or high) to be violated, then buy the sell stops (or sell the buy
stops).
- Displacement and FVG: If no clear swing point violation, look for a sudden, aggressive displacement, then
wait for price to retrace into a Fair Value Gap, and trade from there.
These are the only two patterns you need; avoid gimmicky names or complex setups. It's a very simple,
either-or strategy, with logic supporting each outcome.

Using NASDAQ as an example (NQ):
- At 1:30 PM, observe a swing low, almost matching a previous low, creating relative equal lows (retail
support).
- Price then trades down below these lows, a sudden drop, appearing dynamic and aggressive on a 1
- minute chart (the floor dropping out).
- This is exactly what you look for to buy: buying the sell stops that were resting below those relative equal
lows, anticipating higher prices. This avoids trying to scalp a few ticks with many contracts.

Contrast this approach with micro-scalping (trading a handful of ticks with many contracts and high
anxiety). While some find success, it communicates a lack of understanding of how price truly works and
how it 'books'.
Instead, focus on:
- Trading with the daily range unfolding.
- Being comfortable knowing the market's likely direction based on your analysis.
- Being precise and dialed-in on predictable market behaviors, especially in index futures.

Futures markets, especially index futures, are traded by institutions and professional traders. The
manipulation is present but generally less 'vulgar' or 'ruthless' than in interbank Forex markets. They tend to
be cleaner and more predictable in their delivery.
- Stand aside during volatile periods caused by unannounced news; wait for markets to sync again.
- Avoid summer months (July and August) as they can be 'hit or miss'. The rest of the year offers nice,
systematic markets.
- If you don't understand these basic mechanics, you can still hurt yourself. Focus on one or two quality
trades, not 25-30 micro-scalps.

section_title: DISCIPLINE, PRACTICE, AND LIVE TRADING DISCLAIMER
content:
Identify specific times in the day for setups. Do not:
- Aim for 25-30 micro-scalp trades.
- Give back profits to the market, especially as a beginner.
It is advised not to trade with live funds while learning. While I sometimes show live account trades to prove
concepts, this is not a permanent practice and is for the benefit of students. Practice in demo accounts and
prioritize understanding over immediate live trading. Hopefully, this was insightful.
